# Practice Questions Across Different Difficulty Levels

The course should label practice problems by difficulty (easy, moderate, hard, challenge) because it will help foster better understanding of concepts for students enrolled in the class. 


<!-- This is a comment. Below, you'll see code for inserting an image. To make this image appear, update <custom-path>. To add an image, save it inside the imgs folder of this repository. -->


## Difficulty vs. Understanding

This line graph shows that as perceived difficulty increases, understanding decreases, suggesting that students struggle more as material becomes harder. This supports the idea that offering practice questions at different difficulty levels could help students build understanding more gradually and improve overall comprehension.



## Prior Experience vs. Difficulty

This boxplot highlights the relationship between students’ prior experience and how difficult they perceive the coursework to be, showing that higher experience is generally associated with lower perceived difficulty. This reinforces the idea that labeling practice problems by difficulty could help students engage with material more appropriately. A boxplot format is useful here because it summarizes the range, median, and variation in responses, making it easier to compare groups.

<img src="static/imgs/Image of orior_experience vs. Diffiuclty.png" alt="Image of Comp110 rainbow logo. "  width="500"/>

## Difficulty vs. Quiz Effectiveness

This barplot demonstrates that students who rate the course as more difficult are more likely to view quizzes as less effective. This supports the analysis idea that clearer structure—like labeling practice problems by difficulty—could enhance student understanding and confidence.A barplot is used because it makes it easy to compare responses across different difficulty levels.

<img src="static/imgs/Image of Difficulty vs. quiz effectiveness.png" alt="Image of Comp110 rainbow logo. "  width="500"/>

## Analysis 

Based on the analysis of the data given in the class survey, it is recommended that a tier-based practice problem system be implemented in the COMP 110 course that categorizes exercise problems by difficulty level. The comparison of the “difficulty” and “understanding” metrics show a significant spread in how students perceive the course material. The data specifically indicated that while many students find the load manageable, a notable percentage reported feeling somewhat lost during more complex assignments. By providing structured, difficulty-rated problems, we can minimize this gap, allowing students to build confidence on easier tasks before tackling harder ones. Similarly, it was discovered during our analysis that students who perceive the class load as less difficult also tend to attribute higher effectiveness to the quizzes, which signifies a positive relationship between understanding of the course and quiz effectiveness. The data also supports a direct negative correlation between prior experience and perceived difficulty. By labeling practice problems with difficulty ratings, we can simulate levels of prior experience, thus allowing for both a higher level of understanding and lower perceived difficulty. 

## Possible Refinements 

To refine this idea, the course could implement a quiz at the beginning of each module, categorizing a student's performance into different levels of understanding and could suggest what level of practice problems they should be attempting. This would prevent students from intentionally choosing “easier” practice problems that ultimately don’t challenge the individual’s abilities. It also gives the student an idea of their current capabilities, and prevents them from attempting problems they aren’t yet fully suited for. Future analysis of the data on these quizzes would aid in determining whether they are effective in raising the level of understanding in the course, while also lowering the perceived difficulty.

## Costs and Trade-offs 

There are several costs that may occur in response to this idea. One such cost is the time commitment required to create these practice problems at varying levels of difficulty. For example, the instructional staff will need to spend more time creating these questions as it will require more time to generate and analyze the levels of each question, as well as take additional time to post on the site. In terms of the students actively enrolled in the class, they will likely need to spend more time when completing these questions. Another such cost is accountability when distinguishing which questions to complete. Students will need to conduct self-reflection to determine their coding skill level and identify gaps in understanding, so they can decide which questions, such as moderate versus easy, are better for them to complete. Not only could this inner retrospection be difficult for students, but it can also lead to a trade-off of reinforcing bad study practices for students, as students might consistently choose easier problems and avoid more challenging ones, limiting their opportunities for growth and understanding. When there are no structured questions, students are forced to complete all questions because they do not know which ones could be easy or hard. 

## Validity of Data Analysis 

Overall, I agree with the validity of the data analysis as it shows that as there are several alternative ideas or extensions that can be explored based on the analysis of this idea. One extension could be to examine the formatting of the questions and how that affects their understanding of course concepts. For example, if they think multiple-choice questions are the easiest, does that help them to understand the course concepts more than writing code questions that are harder. This could be analyzed based on student survey responses or by analyzing quiz grades. An alternative idea in comparison to this could be to explore other ways that students can better understand course concepts. For example, determining whether YouTube code tutorial videos, practice problems, or textbooks are better tools for students. A third alternative idea could be to utilize a software that will automatically generate questions based on the students’ performance. For example, if a student gets several questions right in a row, the software will generate harder questions. After the practice session, the software can produce statistics on which concepts the student understood and consistently got right, and which the student should revisit. This idea has been used in other classes, so it makes this idea more logical for the future.
